content.text: Embedding an agent inside a chat platform — Slack, Discord, Microsoft Teams — puts AI capability where teams already work. Users mention a bot, ask a question, or invoke a slash command; the bot calls the agent, and the reply appears in the thread. The integration challenge is platform-specific: signature verification, webhook acknowledgement, rate limits on the posting API, and mapping chat thread IDs to conversation sessions.

attrs.alt: Chat platform agent integration: Slack/Discord → bot gateway → agent (context builder + LLM loop) → tools → reply; Redis session store

content.text: The Slack Bolt SDK handles signature verification, event dispatch, and the 3-second acknowledgement requirement automatically. The bot must reply to Slack's challenge immediately and do the actual agent work asynchronously.

# pip install slack-bolt anthropic redis
import os, threading
from slack_bolt import App
from slack_bolt.adapter.socket_mode import SocketModeHandler
from anthropic import Anthropic
import redis, json

app = App(token=os.environ["SLACK_BOT_TOKEN"])
client = Anthropic()
r = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)

def load_thread_history(channel: str, ts: str) -> list[dict]:
    key = f"slack_thread:{channel}:{ts}"
    raw = r.get(key)
    return json.loads(raw) if raw else []

def save_thread_history(channel: str, ts: str, messages: list[dict]) -> None:
    key = f"slack_thread:{channel}:{ts}"
    r.set(key, json.dumps(messages[-40:]), ex=86400)  # 24h TTL

def run_agent(channel: str, thread_ts: str, user_msg: str, say) -> None:
    history = load_thread_history(channel, thread_ts)
    history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_msg})

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=600,
        system="You are a helpful Slack bot. Keep replies concise and use Slack markdown.",
        messages=history,
    )
    reply = response.content[0].text
    history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
    save_thread_history(channel, thread_ts, history)

    # Post the reply in the same thread
    say(text=reply, thread_ts=thread_ts)

@app.event("app_mention")
def handle_mention(event, say):
    # Bolt acknowledges automatically; run the agent in a background thread
    # so we return from the handler quickly (Slack requires < 3s acknowledgement)
    channel = event["channel"]
    thread_ts = event.get("thread_ts") or event["ts"]  # Use existing thread or start one
    user_msg = event["text"]

    threading.Thread(
        target=run_agent,
        args=(channel, thread_ts, user_msg, say),
        daemon=True,
    ).start()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Socket Mode: no public URL required; bot connects over WebSocket
    handler = SocketModeHandler(app, os.environ["SLACK_APP_TOKEN"])
    handler.start()

When running on serverless infrastructure (AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Run), use Slack's Events API in HTTP mode rather than Socket Mode. The handler must acknowledge within 3 seconds — the standard pattern is to enqueue the event to SQS and return 200 immediately.

# pip install flask slack-sdk anthropic boto3
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify
from slack_sdk.signature import SignatureVerifier
import os, json, boto3

app_flask = Flask(__name__)
verifier = SignatureVerifier(os.environ["SLACK_SIGNING_SECRET"])
sqs = boto3.client("sqs", region_name="us-east-1")
QUEUE_URL = "https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789/slack-events"

@app_flask.post("/slack/events")
def slack_events():
    # Verify Slack's signature to reject forged requests
    if not verifier.is_valid_request(request.get_data(), request.headers):
        return jsonify({"error": "invalid signature"}), 403

    body = request.json
    # Slack sends a one-time URL verification challenge on app setup
    if body.get("type") == "url_verification":
        return jsonify({"challenge": body["challenge"]})

    # Enqueue and acknowledge immediately — never run the agent inline here
    event = body.get("event", {})
    if event.get("type") == "app_mention":
        sqs.send_message(QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL, MessageBody=json.dumps(event))

    return jsonify({"ok": True})

# Separate Lambda / worker processes the SQS message:
# def process_slack_event(sqs_event):
#     for record in sqs_event["Records"]:
#         event = json.loads(record["body"])
#         ... run agent, call chat.postMessage via slack_sdk WebClient

Slash commands give users a structured way to invoke specific agent capabilities without mentioning the bot. Each command is a separate endpoint; Slack POST's the command payload and expects a 200 within 3 seconds.

from slack_bolt import App
from anthropic import Anthropic
import os, threading

app = App(token=os.environ["SLACK_BOT_TOKEN"])
client = Anthropic()

def generate_and_post(ack, respond, command_text: str):
    ack()  # Must call ack() within 3 seconds
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=500,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Summarise this text concisely:\n{command_text}"}]
    )
    # respond() posts an ephemeral message visible only to the user
    respond(text=resp.content[0].text)

@app.command("/summarise")
def handle_summarise(ack, respond, command):
    # Run in thread so ack() can be called before agent finishes
    text = command.get("text", "").strip()
    if not text:
        ack(text="Usage: /summarise <text to summarise>")
        return
    threading.Thread(target=generate_and_post, args=(ack, respond, text), daemon=True).start()

@app.command("/ticket")
def handle_ticket(ack, respond, command):
    ack()
    title = command.get("text", "No title")
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=200,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Write a one-paragraph support ticket for: {title}"}]
    )
    respond(text=f"*Ticket draft:*\n{resp.content[0].text}")
Discord's gateway uses a persistent WebSocket connection. The
library manages connection and dispatches events to async handlers. Unlike Slack, there is no 3-second hard acknowledgement; the bot can
the agent response directly inside the handler (though
shows a typing indicator while the agent runs).

# pip install discord.py anthropic redis
import discord, os, redis as redis_lib, json
from anthropic import Anthropic

intents = discord.Intents.default()
intents.message_content = True
bot = discord.Client(intents=intents)
client = Anthropic()
r = redis_lib.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)

def load_channel_history(channel_id: int) -> list[dict]:
    raw = r.get(f"discord:{channel_id}")
    return json.loads(raw) if raw else []

def save_channel_history(channel_id: int, messages: list[dict]) -> None:
    r.set(f"discord:{channel_id}", json.dumps(messages[-30:]), ex=3600)

@bot.event
async def on_ready():
    print(f"Logged in as {bot.user}")

@bot.event
async def on_message(message: discord.Message):
    if message.author == bot.user:
        return
    if bot.user not in message.mentions:
        return

    text = message.content.replace(f"<@{bot.user.id}>", "").strip()
    if not text:
        return

    async with message.channel.typing():
        history = load_channel_history(message.channel.id)
        history.append({"role": "user", "content": f"{message.author.name}: {text}"})

        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=500,
            system="You are a helpful Discord bot. Be concise. Use Discord markdown.",
            messages=history,
        )
        reply = response.content[0].text
        history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
        save_channel_history(message.channel.id, history)

    await message.reply(reply)

bot.run(os.environ["DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN"])

Both Slack and Discord enforce posting rate limits. Slack allows ~1 message per second per channel; Discord's global rate limit is 50 requests/second. When the agent needs to post a long response, split it into chunks and add a brief delay between posts.

import asyncio

MAX_SLACK_MESSAGE_LEN = 3000  # Slack's limit is 4000 chars; leave headroom

def split_message(text: str, max_len: int = MAX_SLACK_MESSAGE_LEN) -> list[str]:
    """Split long text into chunks that respect Slack's message size limit.""""
    if len(text) <= max_len:
        return [text]
    chunks = []
    while text:
        if len(text) <= max_len:
            chunks.append(text)
            break
        # Try to split at a paragraph boundary
        split_at = text.rfind("\n\n", 0, max_len)
        if split_at == -1:
            split_at = text.rfind("\n", 0, max_len)
        if split_at == -1:
            split_at = max_len
        chunks.append(text[:split_at].strip())
        text = text[split_at:].strip()
    return chunks

async def post_chunked(channel, text: str, delay: float = 0.5):
    for chunk in split_message(text):
        await channel.send(chunk)
        await asyncio.sleep(delay)  # Respect rate limit between chunks
signatures. Always reject requests that fail verification.
— request only the OAuth scopes needed:
Escape user input before tool calls
— a user can type arbitrary text into a chat message. Validate and sanitise before passing to database queries or shell commands.
— without per-user limits, one user can exhaust your LLM budget. Track usage by Slack user ID and enforce a daily cap.
are long-lived credentials. Keep them in AWS Secrets Manager or Kubernetes secrets, not in environment files or source code.

Chat bots that only answer questions get limited adoption. Bots that can take actions — create tickets, look up orders, schedule meetings — become indispensable. Wire tool use into the agent loop and surface tool results in the chat reply.

Slack Block Kit lets the bot send structured interactive messages with buttons, select menus, and modals. This is useful for human-in-the-loop confirmation: the agent drafts an action and asks the user to approve before executing it.
Without per-user limits, one enthusiastic user can exhaust your entire LLM budget. Track usage by platform user ID in Redis and enforce a daily token cap.

import redis, json
from datetime import date

r = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)
DAILY_TOKEN_LIMIT = 50_000  # tokens per user per day

def get_user_tokens_today(user_id: str) -> int:
    key = f"tokens:{user_id}:{date.today()}"
    val = r.get(key)
    return int(val) if val else 0

def record_token_usage(user_id: str, tokens: int) -> None:
    key = f"tokens:{user_id}:{date.today()}"
    r.incrby(key, tokens)
    r.expire(key, 86400 * 2)  # Keep for 2 days

def check_rate_limit(user_id: str, estimated_tokens: int = 1000) -> bool:
    """Return True if the user has budget remaining.""""
    used = get_user_tokens_today(user_id)
    return (used + estimated_tokens) <= DAILY_TOKEN_LIMIT

# Usage in Slack handler:
def handle_with_rate_limit(user_id: str, text: str, say, thread_ts: str) -> None:
    if not check_rate_limit(user_id):
        say(text=":warning: You've reached your daily usage limit. Try again tomorrow.", thread_ts=thread_ts)
        return
    from anthropic import Anthropic
    client = Anthropic()
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=500,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}]
    )
    record_token_usage(user_id, resp.usage.input_tokens + resp.usage.output_tokens)
    say(text=resp.content[0].text, thread_ts=thread_ts)

Chat integrations route platform events (mentions, slash commands) through a bot gateway that verifies signatures and acknowledges within the platform's timeout. Slack Bolt handles this with Socket Mode for always-on bots and webhook mode for serverless. Discord uses a persistent WebSocket via

. Conversation history maps to thread IDs (Slack) or channel IDs (Discord) and persists in Redis with a TTL. Split long responses to stay within message length limits, rate-limit posting between chunks, and enforce per-user token budgets to control cost.
